---
title: "Native Temporal Contract"
subtitle: "Lossless clocks, temporal support, heterogeneous grids, and downstream handoff."
sidebar: spec
---

## Status and scope

This page is the normative temporal-bookkeeping contract for `natural_features`.
The package records when a feature was measured, which clock its coordinates use,
what temporal interval each row represents, and how clocks are related. It does
not require unrelated feature families to share a sampling grid.

The modeling boundary is deliberate: HRF convolution, interpolation onto scan
TRs, lag construction, and design-matrix policy belong in downstream modeling
libraries such as `fmrimod`, `fmrireg`, or `fmridesign`. A TR may be useful
downstream, but it is not part of a native feature's identity here.

## Required temporal fields

Every `FeatureSeries`, `EventSeries`, and `TrackSeries` has:

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `timebase.reference` | The `ClockRef` on which its numeric coordinates are expressed. |
| `timebase.support` | A `SupportSpec` declaring point, fixed-window, or per-row interval support. |
| `temporal_context` | A validated `TemporalContext` containing known mappings among clocks. |
| `times_s` or `onset_s`/`offset_s` | Native coordinates in seconds on the declared clock. |
| `time_bounds_s` | Optional per-row bounds for dense and track data; required by interval support. |

`EventSeries` always carries explicit onset and offset bounds. Dense and track
objects expose `temporal_bounds_s`, which resolves point or fixed-window support
and uses `time_bounds_s` for irregular intervals.

## Clock mappings

`ClockMap(source, target, scale, offset_s)` always means:

$$
t_{target} = scale \times t_{source} + offset_s
$$

The scale must be positive and finite. `TemporalContext` treats mappings as a
graph, resolves inverse and composed paths, and rejects contradictory paths.

For example, if stimulus time 30 s is scan time 7 s:

```{python}
from natural_features import ClockMap

stimulus_to_scan = ClockMap(
    "stimulus",
    "scan:run-01",
    offset_s=-23.0,
)
stimulus_to_scan.apply(30.0)
```

Clock conversion changes coordinates and support widths only. It never changes
row count, interpolates values, or creates a common grid.

## Clock naming conventions

Clock names are identifiers, not an enum, so projects may define the clocks
they actually possess. Recommended names are:

- `stimulus`: coordinates relative to the authored stimulus.
- `media:<id>`: coordinates relative to a particular decoded media stream.
- `experiment`: a continuous experiment-level clock across runs.
- `scan:run-01`: seconds relative to the first acquired volume of one run.
- `run:<id>`: another run-local clock when it is not specifically a scan clock.

The mapping equation determines offset sign. If stimulus 30 s is scan 7 s,
stimulus-to-scan has `offset_s=-23`. The inverse scan-to-stimulus map has
`offset_s=+23`. If stimulus time zero begins 5 s after experiment time zero,
stimulus-to-experiment instead has `offset_s=+5`. Always name both endpoints
and test a known correspondence rather than interpreting an unsigned “delay.”

## Native grids and bundles

`FeatureBundle` is the durable container for heterogeneous temporal outputs. A
100 ms acoustic feature, a 500 ms semantic feature, and irregular word events
remain three native arrays. The bundle owns their shared `TemporalContext` and
can produce a coordinate-transformed view or a `TemporalPayload` for a modeling
consumer.

This is intentionally not represented as one wide table. A rectangular table
would require an explicit target clock and join policy, and may require
resampling. Those are analysis choices, not bookkeeping defaults.

## Alignment and table rules

- `Timeline` and `FeatureAlignment` retain source and target clock references.
- Cross-clock alignment requires a resolvable `TemporalContext` path.
- Alignment records correspondence; it does not interpolate feature values.
- Cross-clock wide joins are rejected unless the caller supplies a temporal
  context, a target clock, and an explicit join policy.
- The only current cross-clock wide-join policy is exact outer matching after
  coordinate conversion. No implicit nearest-neighbor or interpolation policy
  exists.

## Storage and identity

NPZ, Zarr, and Parquet round trips preserve schema, `TimebaseSpec`, support,
clock mappings, coordinates, and explicit temporal bounds. Catalog records and
manifests include the full temporal contract and a `temporal_digest`.

Two artifacts with equal values but different timestamps, support, clocks, or
clock mappings are therefore different artifacts. Time is part of data
identity, not incidental metadata.

## Compatibility boundary

The default clock remains `"stimulus"`, so existing constructors remain valid.
The older `feature_t0_s` and `feature_t0_by_run` fMRI-query arguments remain as
compatibility shorthands. New code should provide explicit `ClockMap` values
through `feature_to_experiment_by_run` or hand native `TemporalPayload` objects
to downstream modeling code.

The registry route `features.hrf` is deprecated. Existing fMRI query and
rendering helpers are compatibility utilities; new pipelines should keep native
features intact here and perform HRF/TR/design operations downstream.

No stable temporal API is removed in compatibility version 2. The stable fMRI
query helpers remain available for that compatibility line; any future removal
requires a compatibility-version increment and a separately documented
deprecation window.

## See also

[Feature object schemas](feature-object-schemas.qmd) · [Keep heterogeneous features on native grids](../cookbook/native-grid-handoff.qmd) · [Feature objects](../concepts/feature-objects.qmd)
